value.market_read: 3-2-1 Crack: The NYMEX 3-2-1 settled at $54.41/bbl, down $0.41 w/w, holding near the top of the recent four-week climb from sub-$48. Wednesday's WPSR for the week ending June 19 showed a 6.09 mb crude draw against builds of 2.06 mb gasoline and 3.06 mb distillate, while refinery utilization ran hot at 96.1% — the product builds against a crude draw trimmed the margin even as WTI itself fell $4.52 on the week to $71.49. With crude weakening more than products on the print, the crack erosion was modest. Watch: Whether sustained 96%+ runs keep rebuilding product cover faster than summer demand can pull it, capping further upside. Gasoline Crack: The RBOB crack settled at $49.76/bbl, off $0.94 w/w, the softest leg of the complex this week. The 2.06 mb gasoline build against firm 96.1% runs signals supply outpacing the post-Memorial Day driving pull, and with WTI down $4.52 the crack compression came from RBOB settlements lagging the crude slide rather than any crude strength. Gasoline exports of 0.76 mb/d remain modest, so retained barrels are weighing domestically. Watch: July driving-season draws — another gasoline build would confirm the demand pull is failing to absorb high-run output. Distillate Crack: The ULSD crack settled at $63.70/bbl, the strongest leg and the anchor of this distillate-led regime. Even with a 3.06 mb distillate build, the crack held firm because diesel inventories remain structurally lean and ULSD exports at 1.39 mb/d are pulling barrels offshore, supporting the front. Freight and trucking demand plus low starting stocks keep the distillate margin well above the gasoline crack despite the seasonal off-peak for heating oil. Watch: Whether refiners shift yield toward distillate at gasoline's expense, and any early Gulf Coast hurricane risk that could spike ULSD cracks. Jet Crack: The jet crack settled at $34.96/bbl, the thinnest margin in the barrel and unsurprisingly so given jet's structurally lower value versus diesel. Summer air-travel demand is providing a floor, but with the distillate-led regime favoring ULSD yield, refiners have limited incentive to swing the regrade toward jet at current spreads. Watch: Peak-summer travel throughput and whether the regrade economics pull any incremental jet yield out of the diesel pool. Brent–WTI Spread: The spread settled at $3.51/bbl, a narrow level that signals a weak US export arb. With WTI down $4.52 w/w to $71.49, the spread is compressed because WTI held relatively firm versus Brent — yet at $3.51 the economics for incremental crude and product exports are thin. Notably crude exports still printed a robust 4.67 mb/d, so existing flows remain intact, but a sub-$4 spread offers little fresh incentive to widen the arb. Watch: The spread needs WTI to fall (or Brent to rise) to re-open export economics; monitor OPEC+ supply signals and Cushing, which drew 1.08 mb to 18.96 mb.
value.chart_321: The 3-2-1 at $54.41 sits in the upper portion of its seasonal band after a steady four-week rally off the sub-$48 lows, consistent with summer-season margin firming. The trajectory has been one of grinding strength even as this week's $0.41 dip marks the first pause, with the move driven more by lean crude balances and 96.1% utilization than by any single product leg. Year-on-year the crack is healthy, underpinned by the distillate-led structure carrying the complex.
value.chart_gas: The gasoline crack at $49.76 slipped $0.94 this week as a 2.06 mb gasoline build undercut the post-Memorial Day driving pull, suggesting high-run output is outpacing demand absorption. With summer-spec gasoline in play and exports at a modest 0.76 mb/d, domestic barrels are accumulating, keeping the gasoline leg the softest in the complex. The seasonal driving season should provide support, but this week's build is a caution flag.
value.chart_dist: The distillate crack at $63.70 remains the dominant leg, defining the distillate-led regime despite a 3.06 mb stock build. Structurally lean diesel inventories and ULSD exports of 1.39 mb/d are draining barrels offshore and supporting the front, with freight and trucking demand underpinning the margin even in the seasonal off-peak for heating oil. This strength is the primary reason refiners are favoring diesel yield over gasoline and jet.
value.chart_jet: The jet crack at $34.96 is the thinnest in the barrel, reflecting jet's structurally lower value versus diesel and the regrade incentive to keep yield in the distillate pool. Summer air-travel demand provides a floor, but at current spreads refiners have little reason to swing toward jet. The gap to the ULSD crack underscores how the distillate-led regime is steering yield decisions.
value.chart_bw: The Brent–WTI spread at $3.51 is narrow, signaling a weak US export arb where incremental crude and product flows are less competitive. Despite the thin spread, crude exports held at a robust 4.67 mb/d, so established flows remain intact — but re-widening the arb would require WTI to fall further or Brent to rise. OPEC+ supply decisions and the 1.08 mb Cushing draw to 18.96 mb are the key swing factors to watch.
